id: es_v2_OVERRIDE_L40_Excessive phone or social media use
scriptId: cs_v2_OVERRIDE_L40_Excessive phone or social media use
historyId: h_v2_OVERRIDE_L40_Excessive phone or social media use
messages: Important update: my employer just informed me the phone I use for both work and personal stuff is managed by their IT system and I can't change its settings.
They won't allow third-party blocking tools or app removals on that device, and IT can push changes remotely.
Also, several channels on that handset must remain audible for client contact, so silencing everything isn't an option.
On top of that I don't have funds to buy a second device or pay for premium services to work around restrictions.
That means a lot of the measures we'd considered that involve altering the phone itself won't be possible now.
I still want to reduce my time, but it has to be done without relying on device-level controls. How should the approach change?
Keep in mind I sometimes need the phone to be on me during the day for work reasons, so leaving it elsewhere isn't always feasible.
I can, however, adjust how I physically handle the device, use other devices like my laptop or an old tablet, and set household routines.
Can you rework the draft six-week plan into one that assumes the main device is off-limits for software changes?
Please focus on no-cost or very low-cost strategies, environmental tweaks, interpersonal agreements, and manual tracking.
A few practical clarifications: I can use kitchen timers, sticky notes, a simple notebook for logging, and place-based cues like a basket or shelf.
I also have a personal laptop and an older tablet that aren't managed; shifting some browsing to them is an option if that helps.
During business hours I still need to respond quickly at times, but evenings and most weekends are where I want the strictest limits.
Include short, friendly sentences I can send when people expect instant replies, and a visible household signal I can use to show I'm off social feeds.
Also add an easy hand-written daily check (two lines max) and a one-step in-the-moment recovery tactic I can use post-slip.

Given the main handset is locked down by workplace policy so I can't alter its software or add helpers, please give me a revised 42-day program that uses only schedule-based, physical, interpersonal and free/manual techniques: daily actions, weekly checkpoints, two practical evening/weekend fallback routines, exact wording to tell contacts I'm limiting replies, a simple paper log format, and a one-paragraph recovery script I can read when I falter.
